data.title: Push And Pull In The Bond Market
data.description:
This is not stagflation! Or so Jay Powell insisted during the Wednesday press conference this week following the Federal Open Market Committee meeting that, as widely expected, kept US policy rates on hold at present levels.

Stagflation was a thing we had to deal with, very harshly, in 1979, when turmoil in Iran was just one of many factors fanning the flames of consumer inflation.

Paul Volcker’s Fed had to raise the Fed funds rate, ultimately to as high as 20 percent, to corral inflation and reinvigorate the economy’s potential to grow. Anything less than the experience of 1979-80, in the world according to Jay Powell, does not merit the term “stagflation.”

Wrong Direction

We’re not in 1979; that much is true. But we do have the two components of that word – the “stag” and the “flation,” if you will - both moving in the wrong direction. On Wednesday we got a report showing wholesale prices in February moving considerably higher than economists had been expecting – and that report reflected activity before hostilities in the Middle East broke out at the end of last month.

Inflation is moving higher while the jobs market is weakening (and Powell and his Fed colleagues believe that actual conditions in the labor market are worse than the already-bad numbers in the jobs reports).

So how is all this affecting conditions in the bond market? The chart below shows the one-year trend for Treasury yields at the two and thirty-year maturities, along with oil prices, noting the before-and-after conditions around the Middle East war.

Since the attacks began on February 28, the 2-year Treasury yield (the blue line on the chart) has jumped from around 3.4 to 3.8 percent, a percentage move of around 12 percent.

The 30-year yield (green line on the chart) has moved from around 4.63 to as much as 4.91 at the same time, a more modest but still solid percentage gain of about 5.8 percent.

Remember that bond yields move inversely to prices, i.e., higher yields mean falling prices. At first glance this might seem counterintuitive. In a risk-off market environment, like the present, Treasuries are supposed to be the refuge, the safe haven where capital flows in search of security.

The problem, of course, is that this is a risk-off environment in which at least several among all the possible outcomes have inflation settling in for a more structurally elevated duration, thus making present nominal yields look relatively unattractive.

Strains on the Upside

The problem with writing these commentaries in the middle of a very fluid and rapidly changing environment is that events can move faster than the hand can type.

As of mid-morning Friday, yields at both the short and long ends of the curve are pushing higher without much apparent hedging sentiment to pull them back. The 30-year is at 4.94 as this sentence is being typed, already some six or seven basis points up from where it started the day.

Although, as the above chart will show, the 30-year yield has been well above this level on a handful of occasions in the past year, so we would still characterize the market as bent but not broken, with at least some safe haven muscle memory to offset the inflationary push.

The short end of the curve is relatively easy to explain: taking their cues from the Fed and from what they see around them, bond investors are backing off from the previous assumption of one or more rate cuts this year (even though the median expectation from the Fed itself, in the Summary Economic Projections released on Wednesday, still calls for one rate cut in 2026). Market yields are adjusting accordingly.

At the long end, where rates are influenced by a wider variety of variables than just monetary policy decisions, the capitulation could start in earnest if the 30-year moves significantly north of five percent.

That would, in our opinion, be a high-probability outcome if the consensus around the war converges on the assumption of no end in sight for the disruption to energy markets.

This week’s bombing of critical energy infrastructure sites in the region has pushed sentiment towards that position. It’s still far short of a universal consensus, but it is also making the best-case scenario of a quick and relatively painless end much less likely.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: monday.com Is Now Another AI Wrapper
data.description:
monday.com (MNDY) is one of many software companies that have been battered down lately by the markets, not because of bad fundamental results, but because of some stormy clouds that can be seen on the company's horizon. Artificial intelligence is here to stay, and it is greatly impacting how the SaaS business environment works. In the case of MNDY, I think that this disruption is collapsing two important pillars of the company: the acquisition channels and the profitability margins due to decreased barriers to entry. For this reason, I consider this stock to be a sell, even at this current low valuation of 12 times forward FCF.

For a company that already had a very tiny moat, if at all, a hit on its acquisition channels is a direct hit to MDNY's long-term growth, while a hit on margins, especially cash flow metrics, is aimed towards valuation, profitability, and also the capacity of reinvesting in marketing to get new growth. Let's get deeper into the MNDY situation.

monday.com: A simple business model

At its core, according to its 10K, MNDY is a software company that sells a tool for work management. This means that team members can see, interact with, and deploy tasks, so everybody is able to follow the workflow around an organization quickly. This software is cloud-based, and it is sticky in nature once MNDY acquires the client, of course.

The pricing model is also straightforward; it is a subscription dependent on how many seats or users are going to have different access categories. While the business is, in general, simple, I consider it still hard to run due to virtually not enough differentiation from competitors at the moment of client acquisition. Competition has been rough across the segment, and is not only coming from platforms very similar in nature, like ClickUp, or Asana (ASAN), but also from companies that began in other scopes, like Atlassian (TEAM) or Notion.

Due to this intense competition and lack of differentiation, these companies usually have a difficult time finding clients. In the case of MNDY, this is expressed in very high $630 million sales and marketing expenses, more than half of the $1.2 billion revenue that the company got in 2025.

In addition to the core work management business, the company is also launching new software to try to compete in the CRM space against companies like Salesforce (CRM) or new initiatives like monday service to compete in the client support segment, and monday dev and monday vibe to make an incursion into the work management in the developer world. I, personally, dislike a lot of these new business segments, since the company is trying to allocate capital to red oceans full of heavy incumbents and new AI menaces. Still, those segments could help to differentiate, or to catch up, its value proposition from its competitors, of course, until they copy MNDY.

MNDY: Now just an AI wrapper

With the disruption coming from AI, there are a lot of questions that arise about how the different SaaS business models would navigate the situation. In the case of monday.com, the implementation inside human workflows has seen the necessity of including AI agents, context chats to ask the AI where to find information on what is going on in the workflow, info modifiers, and compilers like AI blocks that help to automate work functions, extract information from documents or implementations to the new initiatives like the CRM and the customer support ones.

As I view it now, this sounds way more like an AI wrapping model with even less differentiation than before than a high-margin SaaS company from some year ago. This is important because MNDY is charging a fixed amount of money while having to pay a variable amount to AI LLM providers, which will have an inevitable impact on margins sooner or later as customers use the product more and more.

A counterargument to this premise comes from arguing that the LLMs will become cheaper over time, so this expense might become negligible. Nonetheless, competitors might be offering a better AI experience than MDNY's. This alone could drive clients to them more easily over time, especially if competitors ramp up their offering like monday is doing right now, therefore keeping margins squeezed and commoditized, like an undifferentiated AI wrapper.

In fact, because of this disruption in barriers to entry between companies, it is not difficult to think that many vertical software companies, big tech companies like Google (GOOG) or Microsoft (MSFT) could offer their own AI native solutions to work management or any of the other initiatives. I mean, at this point, the entire MNDY's business is very close to being just another new feature of the tech giants, who already have massive distribution.

Those perspectives do not account for other menaces like the advances in "vibe coding" or platforms like base44 or Emergent, that could eventually lead some of the possible new MNDY customers to choose a quick, easy-to-maintain, and easy-to-deploy solution built with their already existing dev teams. I think this scenario is not the principal thesis against MDNY, or any SaaS for that matter, but it is still worthwhile watching because nothing is really stopping that technology from improving to perfection over time.

Another important menace from client acquisition comes from new AI native vertical companies that can offer work management flows built into their offerings. For example, companies like Legora or Harvey, but adapted to other industries like architecture, biology, chemistry, engineering, or material sciences, could make companies like MNDY unnecessary, wiping off its possible growth in these segments.

The red flags are waving

monday.com reported very good financial results in its last quarter, reflecting a year of growth and cash flow. According to the shareholder letter, the company grew 25% in the quarter year over year, although the free cash flow margin adjusted was diminishing.

I think the company's numbers demonstrate that monday.com has a great product, specifically if we see that existing customers are spending more and more money inside the platform.

MNDY has also seen a good adoption of the monday vibe tool, which is partially good news since this service is AI expensive and can lower monday's margins. This also enhances the narrative that this company is becoming just a quick AI wrapper.

Net dollar retention rate is also keeping up, at 110%, boosted mainly by the conversion of existing clients to high-quality clients spending more than $50 thousand in ARR.

Now, the really bad news comes from the client's growth at just 3%. The management comments in the earnings call, that the cost of client acquisition has increased. This is, in my view, a result of all the competition and different new alternatives that customers now have at their disposal. This means that the company's extra $100 million in sales and marketing spending last year, which managed to reach half of the total current revenue, is not working. The management also remarked that this is caused by the disruption in SEO generated by quick responses in AI products like Gemini or ChatGPT.

This situation of not many new customers added to the bigger expenses in marketing, and AI might continue to deteriorate margins, and a cheap free cash flow valuation of the current 12 times might become bigger if margins contract further. Additionally, for the next year, the revenue growth might come at just 19%; this is from the inertia of existing clients upspending inside the platform. I think the product is good, but the client acquisition and the offer have become a commodity.

Another big red flag is that the management is considering changing from the current pay-per-seat model to a payment for consumption, again, solidifying the image as an AI wrapper that is just arbitraging its software between the client and the LLM providers. I am not confident in that model, resulting in a margin expansion from here, even more so with the lack of traction in acquiring new customers.

Balance sheet

From a balance sheet standpoint, the company is quite resilient, having $1.8 billion in current assets against just $860 million in total liabilities. This is reasonable because of the heavy dilution that MNDY has presented across the years, spending out of the shareholders' property rights instead of its own cash.

Just recently, the company announced a plan to buy back $870 million in shares. This is substantial, as it represents around 23% of the total share count. This might help the stock price to stabilize for a while. On the back side, this might indicate that the company couldn't find the best way to put its equity to grow, which means that the management might have given up in the customer acquisition department.

Valuation

I think monday.com has a great product, but unfortunately, that alone is rarely a sufficient reason to consider buying a stock. MNDY might continue to grow by the inertia of its existing clients buying the product, but its margins will likely shrink, and new customers might not be expected.

For that, I provide a valuation model that will keep the current 12 times FCF valuation, the inertia growth at $200 million per year, and lower margins year over year. I cannot calculate how an eventual change in the pricing model from seat-based to consumption would be, since it is still too speculative. Every FCF number is really the adjusted free cash flow that the company reports, which is very close to the traditional one. The decrease in FCF margin calculated will be 4% year over year, agreeing with the fall in 2025 and stabilizing at 12% in 2028. The share count calculation will take into consideration the net $610 million in buybacks that are supposed to happen in 2026, discounting the usual stock-based compensation of around $180 million.

According to the model, the company might not generate capital appreciation from now to 2030. This is because of the constraints in margins that prevent the inertial revenue growth from being translated into cash flow growth for the shareholders.

Despite that, the company might see some appreciation coming from the aggressive buybacks that might erase up to 20% of the company's shares in a very brief amount of time.

Risks

There are some risks to the thesis, which are more inclined to not buy the stock or close a position instead of going directly short or buying put options.

For instance, the company could see client acquisition growth again from its aggressive marketing expenses if the client growth stagnation that we saw last year was caused by some macroeconomic or external issue related to companies' spending less on new software. If that variable changes, the thesis should be reevaluated because the company might have a differential factor that I overlooked.

Takeaway

MNDY has a great product, but unfortunately, that is not enough to create a great business and a stock worth buying. Risks of disruption to this company come from every corner, and while the management is doing what it can to fight against uncertainty, it is still a very difficult stock to own.

data.title: ADX: Attractive For Retirees Seeking Income And Growth (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Overview

The traditional advice for retirees is to follow the 4% rule, but I would argue that this approach is too conservative in this era of modern income tools. Adams Diversified Equity Fund (ADX) offers investors a high dividend yield while providing direct equity exposure to some of the highest quality companies in the world. Now, I am not arguing that an investor should throw all of their eggs into one basket, but I do believe that ADX's 8% dividend yield isn't as scary as it sounds. A position in ADX can be just one of the gears in an investor's diversified portfolio of assets.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that ADX's share price has increased by about 14.8%. However, 2026 has been off to a rough start, so the share price has retreated a bit over the last few months. If we include all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 27.6% over the same time frame. ADX aims to issue a 2% distribution based on its NAV every quarter, which can be attractive for investors seeking a source of reliable dividend income.

When I previously covered ADX, I issued a hold rating due to the fund's premium valuation at the time. Since then, the fund has pulled back alongside the rest of the market, so I wanted to revisit the fund's overall value proposition through 2026. Furthermore, I wanted to highlight some reasons why ADX would be a solid option for retired investors that may be willing to take on more equity risk for a higher yield. We've already seen how the health of the debt markets can crumble during an elevated interest rate environment, so ADX can be a suitable holding for investors that are bullish on the outlook of equities.

While I believe the fund can be a solid long-term holding, there are still some tradeoffs that should be considered. For instance, the success of ADX is ultimately reliant on market indices trading higher over time. The fund may not offer sufficient protection against prolonged declines, and the portfolio is heavily reliant on technology holdings. If the outlook for AI doesn't meet expectations, investors may continue to rotate out of technology, and this will negatively impact ADX's NAV growth.

Strategy Isn't Riskier Than A S&P 500 ETF

According to the latest fund overview, ADX has total net assets of $3B that are allocated across a diverse range of equities. The fund has a reasonable expense ratio of 0.49% of managed assets, which is worth it considering the active management of the portfolio. The underlying strategy is simple because ADX essentially mirrors the makeup of the S&P 500 Index (SPX). However, management actively selects specific stocks that it believes can achieve the alpha needed to support that higher dividend yield offered.

I think that ADX is a great fit for retirees because the underlying strategy is very straightforward and easy to understand. The fund doesn't utilize any sort of fancy option strategies and avoids debt securities as part of its positioning. Additionally, ADX avoids using any leverage, so the fund will not experience any accelerated downside risks or higher operational costs related to this leverage. The fund relies strictly on the alpha that it is able to provide by skewing the weighting heavier towards stocks that management believes to be strong.

Since the fund is made up of the same constituents as the S&P 500, the breakdown by sector looks very similar. For instance, ADX is most heavily skewed towards technology holdings at 34.15% of total assets. This heavier allocation towards technology may come off as risky, but I want to remind investors that a lot of retirees following the 4% rule are allocated to S&P 500-focused funds or total stock market funds, which are both heavily skewed towards technology as well. So what's really the difference here?

However, you may already know that many traditional equities do not provide a dividend yield high enough to support ADX's high dividend yield. So in order to keep up with the high payouts, ADX has to sell off the upside gains of its assets over time. So if one of ADX's holdings experiences a rapid upward move, management may trim this position and take those capital gains to support the distributions.

When looking at the top holdings of ADX, we can see that investors are getting direct exposure to some of the highest quality companies in the world. For instance, the top position is currently Nvidia (NVDA) with a weight of 7.87%. Once again, I want to remind investors that the State Street S&P 500 ETF (SPY) is also heavily weighted in Nvidia at 7.31% of total assets, so I want to challenge the idea that ADX is any more risky than a more standard ETF. Investors are also getting exposure to other high quality companies, including Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet (GOOG), Amazon (AMZN), and Broadcom (AVGO), just to name a few.

Outlook

I will admit that ADX is very top-heavy since its top ten positions make up nearly 43% of total assets. Furthermore, ADX has fewer positions than standard S&P 500 funds, so it is taking a more focused approach on the technology sector. For this reason, I would not suggest a position in ADX for investors that aren't optimistic about the growth of AI over the next decade. As many of ADX's top holdings are the large-cap leaders in the expansion of the AI sector, the fund will certainly be impacted by which direction sentiment heads over the next few years.

The biggest issue right now is that technology companies are selling off due to concerns around the threat of AI. This is the ironic way of the world sometimes: only a few quarters ago we saw companies spike higher whenever AI was mentioned on an earnings call or quarterly release. Now, AI has become so advanced that investors are worried about AI eliminating the moat for many SaaS companies. Furthermore, companies continue to implement mass layoffs as they allocate capital towards record-breaking capex levels to build out the infrastructure needed to support growing demand. Just for context, here are the estimated capex amounts for some of the leaders across the tech sector:

  • Amazon: ~$200B
  • Alphabet: ~$175B-$185B
  • Microsoft: ~$140B
  • Meta Platforms: ~$115B-$135B

I am optimistic that these companies will eventually shift back into a growth phase at some point, which means that ADX is directly aligned to participate in this expansion of the market. For instance, Grand View Research estimates that the AI market will be worth ~$3.5T by 2033. This represents a potential CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 30.6% going forward. Even if this estimate is off by 50%, this would still represent a double-digit growth outlook over the next few years.

I would argue that retirees are better off getting exposure to the growth of AI through the large-cap leaders that have huge businesses with many different legacy streams of revenue. This is a much safer way to get exposure to the technology sector compared to the speculative small- and mid-cap companies that don't have established business segments. Despite my optimism, there is always a chance that sentiment in the technology sector shifts and the growth of AI rapidly slows over the next few years. Perhaps companies aren't able to justify these large capex spending levels, and the rate of earnings growth is disappointing.

After reviewing the most recent annual report for the 2025 period, I believe that ADX will continue to grow its underlying NAV as long as we don't face a severe and prolonged downturn. As established, the fund is heavily reliant on its ability to capture net realized gains since there aren't any income focused segments within the portfolio. ADX reported earnings of $3.83 per share through 2025, which was enough to support distributions and support NAV growth.

However, the issue arises when the fund cannot capture the necessary level of net realized gains. A good example of this issue was demonstrated back in 2022. ADX experienced total losses of $3.99 per share, which meant that it did not generate enough income to support distributions. As a result, the NAV fell, and it took two years for the fund to fully recover from that loss. Just for reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2021: $22.50 per share
  • 2022: $17.38 per share
  • 2023: $20.56 per share
  • 2024: $22.64 per share
  • 2025: $24.72 per share

Since the fund essentially mirrors the makeup of the S&P 500, valuation is a bit tricky to get right. The fund is structured as a closed-end fund, but the growing valuation of the indices means that ADX's typical price-to-NAV has also increased. For instance, ADX now trades at a small discount to NAV of 4.16%. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that this is nearly the lowest price-to-NAV level over the last ten year range.

So as market indices trend downward, I believe that we are now getting the opportunity to enter at a more reasonable valuation. Going forward, I believe that ADX's price to NAV valuation will typically trade at a much smaller discount level due to its consistent performance over a longer holding period. With the growing demand of investors seeking supplemental income sources, I believe that ADX actually has the chance to eventually trade in the premium to NAV territory. For instance, data suggests that equity income ETFs saw a record $97B of inflows through 2025, since investors want income without completely abandoning equities.

ADX is a bit of a unicorn because it is a closed-end fund that has been able to provide an attractive combination of income and capital appreciation. Looking at the performance over the last decade, we can see that ADX's share price has increased by 76.66%. When including all distributions paid, the total return jumps to 331.2% over the same time frame. I would consider this a success for retirees that want to maintain equity exposure and collect income.

Lastly, ADX's distributions have proven to help the fund provide a bit more resilience during market declines. While this isn't always the case, and it ultimately depends on how severe of a downside we experience, this can be a great aspect for retirees looking for a bit of protection. For instance, ADX's performance on a YTD basis sits at loss of 2.14%, which is slightly better than SPY's total loss of 3.24% over the same time frame.

Dividend Vulnerability

As of the latest declared quarterly distribution of $0.47 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 8.4%. However, the fund specifically states that the targeted payouts will amount to 2% of the NAV every quarter. So if the indices continue to decline downward like they have over the last few months, I anticipate that ADX's next distribution will be a bit lower. Therefore, investors should consider the risk that ADX's distributions can be impacted by unfavorable market conditions, especially if we go through a multi-year decline. However, the good news is that management is incentivized to grow the fund's NAV over time because their fees are tied to total assets under management.

So since the distributions are tied to the NAV, this acts as an incentive to fix performance. A lower payout during poor performing years means that ADX may be able to have excess capital to deploy once the eventual recovery takes place. For retirees, the 8% rule is sustainable, but the actual dollar amount may fluctuate over time. The good news is that this works in your favor during periods where the underlying NAV grows as well.

Furthermore, ADX is great for retirees since the distributions may be more tax-efficient than high-yield counterparts. For instance, the latest section19(a) notice indicates that the majority of the payout was funded by return of capital, which isn't classified as income and therefore isn't taxed as such. The caveat is that return of capital distributions are typically used when a fund fails to generate the earnings needed to support payouts. Under more normal market conditions, the fund has historically leaned on net realized long-term gains, which are classified as qualified dividends and have favorable tax consequences for investors.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am upgrading my rating on ADX at this time. Despite the higher price-to-NAV valuation, I believe that the fund continues to offer compelling value for retirees that want income without completely abandoning the equities market. Managers have a long track record of achieving alpha through stock selection and weighting. Furthermore, the straightforward strategy avoids leverage or any fancy debt securities, which increases its appeal for long-term investors. The straightforward 2% distribution per quarter means that the fund's payouts will be self-correcting during unfavorable market periods, which can help soften downside risks. Lastly, the fund has the potential to deliver tax-efficient income for retirees.

data.title: Commodity Price Watch: March 2026
data.description:
What is the overall trend in commodity prices?

The outbreak of the war in the Middle East at the end of February and beginning of March turned a relatively sedate 2026 outlook into one fraught with uncertainty.

As a general statement, energy prices are now strongly higher. Chemicals are heavily dependent on oil and gas, so also see upward revision. Aluminum is strongly impacted because of supply disruption, while steel is moderately affected and copper only indirectly through demand implications. The longer the duration of the war, the greater the exhaustion of existing inventory and thus much greater supply and price consequences. The war has taken attention away from protectionism, but the issue has not gone away.

As measured by the Materials Price Index (MPI) by S&P Global Market Intelligence, industrial materials prices bottomed in the second quarter of 2025 and will revisit those levels in the second quarter of this year. For the fourth quarter of 2025, the MPI was 5% below its year-earlier level. Prices are returning to year-earlier levels, however, as the MPI for the second quarter of 2026 is forecast to be 0.4% below the previous year.

Regionality rather than global is a commodity market feature that has grown in recent years, and the war will exacerbate the trend. Regions self-sufficient in energy — particularly oil and gas — will have less direct impact, while importers will face higher energy costs and thus dampened demand in other parts of the economy.

What is our forecast for energy-related commodity prices?

War in the Middle East sent oil prices soaring. The eventual price trajectory will depend on the length of time that little to no traffic passes the Strait of Hormuz. The base case presented here assumes about two weeks of near closure of the strait. Daily Brent crude pricing has already exceeded $100/barrel, but once traffic resumes in the strait, the price will come down slightly.

Infrastructure damage will mean that even when traffic resumes in the strait, 1 million to 2 million barrels/day of production will remain offline through the end of the second quarter. Risk remains high. This timeline is just one of many possibilities, and if the Strait of Hormuz sees little traffic for months rather than weeks, prices could go well above $100/b.

Gasoline and diesel prices will jump in line with oil. Gasoline prices average just below 400 cents per gallon for the second and third quarters — kept elevated by seasonal factors despite crude declining, meaning our advice for the second quarter is to buy as needed. Diesel prices will decline in the third quarter.

US natural gas is the only major energy commodity with virtually no upward pricing pressure from the war in the Middle East. Export growth continues to lead pricing gains, but global LNG prices were already such that the US was exporting as much as it could.

What is our forecast for chemical prices?

There is a broad-based increase in the short-term pricing outlook across the board. The war has caused substantially higher feedstock cost pressures, as well as global supply disruptions. The biggest increases are being seen in the Asia region, followed by Europe and then North America.

Still, there is nowhere to run or hide, as higher spot pricing is being seen globally. Price rises across petrochemicals in the first half of 2026 will be broad, mirroring crude oil and natural gas increases.

What is our forecast for steel prices?

Steel prices are higher in the US and Europe while little changed in most of Asia. The US has very little direct impact from the war, but disruption to shipping is expected to restrict imports. US mills will not lower prices until imports undercut domestic market power.

European electric furnace steelmaking is strongly exposed to electricity prices. The loss of natural gas from the Gulf will cause higher electricity prices that will be partially passed on to steel pricing. Weak demand will prevent the full pass-through of higher input costs.

Mainland Chinese prices will be mostly flat as output exceeds consumption while global trade in steel is underwhelming. Supply disruption is unlikely but may occur if war-related shipping issues arise, or if temporary energy shortages curtail production.

Demand will be flat to negative in Europe, the United States and mainland China. Demand was already forecast as flat at best, and the war will undercut even more. East Asia and India do grow, but they are dependent on Middle East oil and gas supplies, so the longer the war, the weaker the outlook.

What is our forecast for nonferrous metal prices?

Prices for aluminum and copper have been revised up through the middle of 2026 because of the ongoing war in the Middle East.

Aluminum prices are elevated on a tight market, which is further constricted by the reduction of smelting capacity because of the conflict with Iran. Higher costs, mainly due to elevated electricity costs, will also feed into prices to bring a significant spike over the first and second quarters of 2026.

Copper prices continue to be driven by short-term effects, even as fundamentals become balanced and a small surplus develops. The return of mainland Chinese investors from the Lunar New Year holiday brought increased speculation, but reduced activity from both industrial and financial buyers has seen prices come under pressure, despite pronounced volatility in the downward trend. However, this has been upended by the start of the war in the Middle East, with increased risk and higher costs feeding into prices.

Although prices are higher, they have recently risen more sharply in response to more modest developments. Price rises may be subdued as companies assess developments, and so there is increased risk that prices may spike upward more than forecast.

This article was published by S&P Global Market Intelligence and not by S&P Global Ratings, which is a separately managed division of S&P Global.

data.title: Funko: The Storm Has Passed
data.description:
The last few days have been a particularly brutal time for shareholders of toy company Funko (FNKO). Since management announced financial results for the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year on March 12th, the stock has fallen 21.6%. Because of this, shares are now down 17.2% since I reaffirmed the company as a ‘strong buy’ candidate back in September of last year. This is disappointing to say the least, especially considering that the S&P 500 is down just 2.5% over that window of time. But honestly, I think the market is reading this all wrong. While the company certainly has certain issues that need to be contended with, management is now making progress on some important areas. Net debt is dropping. Inventories are pulling back. Yes, revenue is declining. But management is also forecasting stable or even growing revenue for this year while also pushing for significant profit improvement. Add on top of this how cheap the stock is, and I firmly believe that maintaining it as a ‘strong buy’ candidate is the right move.

Mr. Market is wrong on this one

After the market closed on March 12th, the management team at Funko announced financial results for the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Over the ensuing days, shares of the company dropped 21.6%. At first glance, you might think that this was because of worsening results. But by almost every measure that matters, the company is showing signs of life. It is true that revenue dropped year over year, falling from $293.7 million to $273.1 million. However, overall revenue ended up being $12.4 million above what analysts were hoping to see.

One surprising data point provided by management involved sales of its Loungefly product line. Revenue actually inched up 1.8%, from $42.4 million to $43.1 million. In the past, management treated this like any other random product line. But in the latest earnings call, the firm said that they have finally put a general manager in charge of it. The belief that management has is that the wearable storytelling market opportunity is meaningful and that the company's fan base for this product line is passionate. For those not familiar with it, Loungefly refers to backpacks, wallets, pins, shirts, and other products that all match its strategy of promoting major brands such as Harry Potter, Hello Kitty, KPop Demon Hunters, and more. The fact that revenue is growing in what can only be described as a difficult market is fascinating.

By comparison, the rest of the company isn't doing all that great. The most significant drop involved its core collectible category, with revenue declining 5% from $232.7 million to $221 million. This is surprising to an extent. I say this because the company has a lot going for it. In its latest investor presentation, as an example, the company highlighted that the second largest property that it is working with right now is none other than One Piece. This is a brand that I am intimately familiar with. In fact, news just broke earlier this year that One Piece finally surpassed Superman as the most popular comic series in history, with over 600 million copies sold. Netflix (NFLX) recently released the second season of the live-action version of the series. A second animated series that starts the story over from scratch, despite the fact that the first animated series is over 1,000 episodes long and still ongoing, is in the works. So the fact that management has such a significant property under its belt is phenomenal.

That's not all. There are plenty of others as well, including another Netflix series known as Stranger Things. It also boasts other arrangements, such as those involving Pokémon, KPop Demon Hunters, Marvel, Harry Potter, and more. Speaking of Pokémon, management mentioned in their latest earnings call that the company is currently the second-largest collectible brand by market share after Pokémon. However, overall revenue suffered because of broader economic conditions and tariff-related matters. The company incurred about $40 million of tariff costs in 2025, around half of which were IEEPA tariffs that were recently ruled unconstitutional. Although that has been replaced by a global tariff rate of 15%, the likely outcome will be an improvement on its bottom line moving forward.

Because of tariffs and broader economic weakness here at home, revenue in the US actually ended up being 12% lower than it was a year earlier. It ultimately fell from $178.2 million to $156.7 million. All international revenue, other than European revenue, dipped only 3.4% by comparison. And in Europe, revenue actually inched up 1.6%, from $94.7 million to $96.2 million. That continent seems to be benefiting from certain Funko product lines, such as Bitty Pop! and Pop! Yourself, both of which were launched in the final quarter of last year.

On the bottom line, the company did see some improvement. The firm went from a loss per share of $0.03 to pretty much breaking even on a per share basis. That meant that the company exceeded analysts’ estimates to the tune of $0.04. That translated to an improvement on the bottom line from a loss of $1.5 million to a loss of only $0.2 million. However, adjusted net profits did worsen, with the per share reading for them dropping from $0.08 to $0.05. That meant a decline in adjusted net income from $4.4 million to $2.5 million. But the good news was that, even with this drop, adjusted earnings per share ended up being $0.02 above what analysts were hoping for. Other profitability metrics declined without exception. Operating cash flow plummeted from $59.8 million to $28.1 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a fall from $21.8 million to $17.7 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business contracted from $26.3 million to $23.3 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for 2025 in their entirety compared to 2024. With a trajectory like this, you might think that I am crazy for being bullish about the company. However, management said that they expect revenue this year to be flat at worst and up 3% at best. In addition to continued momentum associated with the product lines that I mentioned already, the company said that 2026 looks to be the strongest entertainment slate that they have seen in the last four or five years now. On the movie front, we have plenty of highly anticipated releases such as Mandalorian and Grogu, Toy Story 5, the Moana live-action film, Spider-Man: Brand New Day, Avengers: Doomsday, and Minions 3. I also mentioned season 2 of the One Piece live action series. All of this is conceptually positive for the company so long as execution works out. It is worth noting that while Loungefly did incredibly well in the final quarter of the year, they do actually expect its sales to drop at a double digit rate because of management's decision to cut SKUs. However, they do expect a high single-digit growth rate when it comes to the company's core collectibles product lines.

More important than the revenue figure is the fact that management anticipates significant improvement in profitability. Tariff mitigation strategies that were implemented last year, including price hikes and certain cost-cutting initiatives, will prove instrumental in pushing EBITDA up from $26.6 million in 2025 to between $70 million and $80 million this year. If we take the midpoint of this, as well as management's forecast for interest expense of $18 million, then a good proxy for adjusted operating cash flow would be about $57 million. Using these estimates, we can see in the chart above how shares are priced on a forward basis. This looks much better than how the company is priced using 2025 figures.

There are some other things to take into consideration. Management has been working hard recently to cut debt. For instance, in the third quarter of last year, the company had net debt of $201.8 million. That was down from the $207.5 million that the company had in the second quarter. But by the final quarter of 2025, it had fallen to $183.2 million. Other than the final quarter of 2024, this was the lowest net debt reading that the company has had for a final quarter of a fiscal year in several years now. And with cash flow likely to be positive in 2026, that should fall further moving forward.

Thanks to this reduction in leverage, as well as management successfully amending and extending through the end of next year its existing credit agreement, it is no longer considered a ‘going concern’ risk in the near term. This means that management does not have substantial doubt about the company's ability to survive over the next 12 months. On top of this, management continues to make progress in reducing inventory. Inventories amounted to $83.1 million at the end of the year. That's down from the $92.6 million reported a year earlier and compares nicely to the $99.8 million reported for the third quarter. It's unclear whether management will reduce this further or not. All that they said in their latest earnings call was that they feel that they are in a healthy place right now and that they are seeing restocking at retailers take place.

Takeaway

The way I see it, the storm seems to have passed for Funko. The company is attractively priced on a forward basis, and operations are stabilizing. Revenue has a chance to grow slightly this year, with the core collectibles part of the business anticipated to rise. Net debt is falling, and inventories have dropped. With management expecting meaningful cash flow this year, there are plenty of reasons to be bullish. For all of these reasons, I believe that maintaining it as a ‘strong buy’ candidate is the appropriate move.

Promising Aristocrats Right Now

This month I'm also adding another factor to this analysis: momentum. Specifically looking for Dividend Aristocrats with strong momentum relative to SPY.

Valuation is assessed using dividend yield theory, which compares the current dividend yield to the 5-year rolling average. The potential long-term return is calculated as the sum of expected future earnings growth, the forward dividend yield, and a potential boost from mean reversion. Momentum is measured using a combination of the 3-, 6-, and 12-month price movement, excluding the last month in the 12-month figure. I measure a composite momentum score and compare that to SPY.

As of March 19th, 39 Dividend Aristocrats appear to be both potentially undervalued and have a long-term annualized expected rate of return of at least 10%. For perspective, as of February 26th, only 25 Dividend Aristocrats appeared to be opportune investment ideas using this screening process.

data.title: U.S. IPO Weekly Recap: REIT Carve-Out Sees Solid Demand While Drone Micro-Cap Soars 500%+
data.description:
Three IPOs priced this past week, joined by two SPACs, and one major issuer joined the pipeline.

Janus Living (JAN) upsized and priced at the high end to raise $840 million at a $5.1 billion market cap. Cornerstone investors had indicated on $300 million of the offering (36% of the deal). Carved out of Healthpeak Properties (DOC), this senior housing REIT owns 34 senior living communities across 10 states, primarily in Florida and Texas. The company is poised to benefit from tailwinds like the aging population in its core markets, though these markets also expose it to climate issues. Janus finished up 18%.

AIM-listed Guardian Metal Resources (GMTL) raised $60 million at a $545 million market cap. Guardian is an exploration-stage mining company focused on tungsten and other minerals in Nevada. Its main projects are the Pilot Mountain and Tempiute tungsten properties, both located in historic mining districts with prior third-party production. The company is still early stage and likely won't begin production for several years. Guardian finished flat.

Military-focused drone software developer Swarmer (SWMR) priced at the midpoint to raise $15 million at a $117 million market cap. Swarmer’s core product, the Trident OS, serves as a software layer integrated into drones to support varying levels of autonomy based on mission requirements. The company is small and highly unprofitable but operating in a hot space. Swarmer popped more than 500% on its first day of trading and was up more than 1,000% on day two; it finished the week up 634%.

Week Ahead

One IPO and one direct listing are currently scheduled in the week ahead, although some smaller issuers may join the calendar throughout the week.

Canadian royalty acquirer The Metals Royalty Co. (TMCR) plans to complete its direct listing on the Nasdaq. The company is engaged in the acquisition and management of critical metals and mineral royalties, streams, and other similar interests. The company's core and only asset is the NORI Royalty, a 2.0% gross overriding royalty on the NORI Property, which is a polymetallic nodule project being developed by The Metals Company (TMC) in the Clarion-Clipperton Zone of the northeast Pacific Ocean that is not currently permitted for commercial production. To date, the company has not received any royalty revenue from the NORI Royalty. Based on the value of its December 2025 private issuance, the company would command a market cap of $292 million.

Clinical-stage immuno-oncology biotech Salspera (TKVA) is tentatively scheduled to raise $85 million at a $938 million market cap in the week ahead. Salspera aims to develop a new class of immunotherapeutics for the treatment of solid tumors, which it refers to as "live biopharmaceuticals," or genetically modified biological agents that have the capability of expressing select anti-tumoral agents within the tumor’s microenvironment. Lead program Saltikva is an attenuated strain of Salmonella Typhimurium that is non-toxic and orally administered. The company is initially targeting pancreatic cancer, having conducted a Phase 2 trial for patients with Stage IV metastatic pancreatic cancer.

data.title: Itochu: A Great Collection, But Expecting Logistic Cost Impacts And JPY Risks
data.description:
ITOCHU Corporation (ITOCY)(ITOCF) (or best through the Japanese listing, which is what we're considering, (ISIN:JP3143600009) has a lot of non-resource businesses, and there's a lot of quality in the profile - we're especially impressed by the marquee FamilyMart brand, which is a very competitive convenience chain and stands out among competitors in some aspects of its food offering. While Marubeni (MARUY) may have a little more drive in the current market due to leverage to resource prices that are going up as a consequence of the crisis, Itochu rings more bells secularly for us and they do have a good deal of relevant resource exposure as well. They are also marginally cheaper on a trailing basis, but we'd say broadly in line with trading company peers when thinking about the sort of incremental earnings we can expect in the coming quarter. But we do want to highlight that a thinner margin business like convenience stores should feel the logistics price increases expected, and input costs will rise. Moreover, we are not optimistic about the JPY. We think they can pilot their results reasonably well, and there are resource offsets, but it's not a super clear path either.

Results

To rattle off some comments, we'd highlight that the oil crisis could have some slightly negative consequences on some of the businesses.

  • Logistics costs could be upward of 6% of revenues for the food segment. Convenience fuel costs could be higher than supermarket and other big box peers on account of more start-stop city logistics and travel and less bulk effect from longer distance highway connectivity, relatively speaking. These businesses aren't so high margin. Currently, we are looking at around 20% increases in prices at the pump, and it's a lot worse for diesel, where the refinery capacity hits associated with Hormuz matter more and are important for trucks, which are more likely to run on diesel. We expect considerable feeling in that business due to the thinner margins, with forecasts already looking at some deceleration before the effects of the crisis are considered. Could shave off a few percent from forecast operating profit growth just from here. Dole could also be a bit affected by higher logistics costs. There may not be pricing power since the domestic buyer will be watching their wallets in Japan with imported inflation in effect and higher fuel prices.

  • For textiles, there is a manufacturing exposure here. We expect some petrochemical pressure on input costs to the apparel profits here, as synthetic fibres are dependent on crude prices and the petrochemical intermediate assets. It could take the wind out of the underlying Descente sales growth.
  • For minerals and metals, there is exposure to coal. That's up in correlation to higher energy prices associated with the oil crisis. That segment could see around 10% recovery based on just the declines in profits in that segment as of Q3, where coal had been down, with coal now up. Additional boosts from turnarounds.

  • In energy and chemicals, intermediate production could see a boost, as will the direct exposure to oil E&P. A percent overall result seems conceivable considering the more than 60% increase in crude spot prices. Higher input costs for iron ore are seeing some end price increases as well, but we don't expect much additional contribution here other than perimeter and underlying operational improvement effects.

  • Construction machinery is a little bit of a concern, though we don't expect much additional pressure on the power business, at least, and the secular growth to continue there.

  • Japan's capital cost considerations are a little unique, but the crisis is definitely inflationary and not a positive. The realty segment isn't particularly well positioned in that regard. Forecasts aren't great there anyway, though.

Bottom Line

The non-resource businesses are interesting. While there may be a generalized cost pressure on them due to the oil crisis (we think maybe more on thinner margin food), tourist volumes (everyone is going to Japan these years) for convenience stores could be an outperformance, and we expect a couple of points in operating profit growth just from the revision vector in energy prices going up rather than staying flat. They have some discretion in piloting their businesses properly. Two coking coal projects are also in turnarounds, as well as Fuji Oil. They are going to benefit from the new crisis situation and were already expected to explain net growth in profits just on the operational turnaround. We don't expect a major upset in overall business performance, but logistic and freight costs are going up; it won't be irrelevant, and we are waiting to see some changes in assumptions as to what will be the ultimate result driver into the FY with less in food, maybe in textiles, and more in the energy and mineral businesses. Power should be fine too.

Also, while the cost of capital may stay low in Japan as opposed to elsewhere, the JPY doesn't do well when oil prices are high and the dependent Japanese economy needs to buy at high stickers on the barrels. JPY has already been marginal, and higher benchmark rates abroad are not a help either with renewed threats of carry trades and generally weaker yield appeal relative to cash safe havens in the current market. Itochu is very domestically focused. Marubeni should be a little heavier on the leverage to the crisis, both with fertilizers and also coal and crude exposure. Maybe that's why it has the premium on PE TTM, 18.5x relative to 16.2x PE for Itochu. Still, we like Itochu and its collection of businesses, but we are thinking about direction and that the forecast profit growth is probably not assured anymore. In fact, just a crimp on food margins could eliminate the planned growth. With a percent or two coming back from minerals and metals possibly, we don't think it will be a full offset.

data.title: Philadelphia Fed Manufacturing Index: Activity Continued To Expand In March
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The Philly Fed's Manufacturing Business Outlook Survey is a monthly survey of about 250 manufacturers in the Third Federal Reserve District, which covers eastern Pennsylvania, southern New Jersey, and Delaware. Participants of the survey indicate the relative level of general business conditions in the region. Since this is a diffusion index, negative readings indicate contraction, positive ones indicate expansion. While it focuses exclusively on business in this district, this regional survey gives a reliable direction of the broader Chicago Fed's National Activity Index.

The latest Philadelphia Fed manufacturing index showed activity continued to expand in March, with the index rising 1.8 points to 18.1. The latest reading marked the highest reading for the index in the last six months and was better than the forecast of 8.3.

Here is the summary from the survey:

Manufacturing activity in the region continued to expand overall, according to the firms responding to the March Manufacturing Business Outlook Survey. The survey’s indicator for general activity remained elevated. The new orders index moved down but remained positive, and the shipments index moved higher. The employment index returned to positive territory but continued to suggest mostly steady employment overall. Both price indexes rose this month after declining last month. The firms continue to expect overall growth over the next six months.

The first chart below gives us a look at this diffusion index since 2000, which shows us how it has behaved in proximity to the three 21st-century recessions. The green dots show the indicator itself, which is quite noisy. Therefore, we've included the three-month moving average (purple line), which is more useful as an indicator of coincident economic activity. We can see longer and deeper periods of contraction during each of the recessions, with shallower contractions in '02, '03, '11, '12, '13, and '15. More recently, the index's 3-month moving average contracted from July 2022 to December 2023; however, no recession was called during that time.

In the next chart, we see the complete series, which dates from May 1960. For proof of the high volatility of the headline indicator, note that the average absolute monthly change across this data series is 8.2.

The next chart is an overlay of the General Activity Index and the Future General Activity Index — the outlook six months ahead.

Firms Continue to Expect Growth

The diffusion index for future general activity declined 3 points to 40.0 this month (see Chart 1). More than 56 percent of the firms expect an increase in activity over the next six months, exceeding the 16 percent that expect a decrease; 23 percent expect no change. The future new orders index fell 5 points to 49.6, while the future shipments index rose 6 points to 53.6. The firms continue to expect increases in employment over the next six months, and the future employment index jumped 26 points to 40.4. Both future price indexes remained above their long-run averages: The future prices paid index ticked down from 54.1 to 53.7, and the future prices received index fell 12 points to 38.4. The index for future capital expenditures rose 11 points to 25.8.

For comparison, here is the latest ISM Manufacturing survey.

Let's compare all five Regional Manufacturing indicators. Here is a three-month moving average overlay of each since 2004 (for those with data).

Here is the same chart including the average of the five. Readers will notice the range in expansion and contraction between all regions.

ETFs associated with industrials and manufacturing include: First Trust Industrials/Producer Durables AlphaDEX Fund (FXR), Industrial Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLI), Vanguard Industrials ETF (VIS), and iShares U.S. Industrials ETF (IYJ).

data.title: Humanoid Robotics ETF: Top Performing Stocks In The KOID Portfolio
data.description:
For investors seeking broader exposure to AI, robotics, and technology, KOID sits within KraneShares’ AI, Robotics & Emerging Technology ETF suite, which holds complementary strategies that focus on global AI and technology.

The stock market is continuously looking forward. Whether it’s an individual company’s earnings guidance or a new emerging industry, investors want to know what’s next. While no one can predict future developments, some investors view humanoid robotics as an emerging area of interest within the broader technology landscape.

That’s why KraneShares launched its equal-weighted Humanoid Robotics ETF, the KraneShares Global Humanoid Robotics and Embodied Intelligence Index ETF (KOID). With KOID, investors gain exposure to the humanoid robotics ecosystem, including the “brain” of the humanoid (semiconductors & technology), the “body” (actuation systems, mechanical systems, sensing & perception, critical materials), and humanoid “integrators” (the companies actually manufacturing humanoids).

KOID also provides investors with access to stocks listed globally. Many humanoid robotics-focused companies are not traded on US exchanges and may be harder for some investors to access, which we believe is a key benefit to a global basket approach used in the KOID humanoid robotics ETF.

What are the 10 top-performing humanoid robotics stocks in the KOID portfolio?

Previously, we covered 4 humanoid robotics stocks building the next-generation humanoid body in the KOID ETF: MP Materials (MP)(critical materials), Rainbow Robotics (mechanical systems), Robosense Technology (RBSTF)(sensing & perception), and UBTech Robotics (UBTRF)(manufacturer). In this article, we highlight the 10 top-performing humanoid robotics stocks in the KOID portfolio since its inception on 6/4/2025, excluding the 4 previously covered stocks.

1. China Northern Rare Earth – 2.43% Weight in KOID as of 2/28/2026, +158.92%% return since KOID inception1

China Northern Rare Earth is a China-based mineral and precious stone mining company that produces rare-earth concentrates, metals, oxides, and new rare-earth materials primarily used in emerging technologies. China Northern plays a key role in the humanoid ecosystem, as its products are needed to build specific body parts and components for robots.

The CEO of China Northern's fellow KOID rare earth company, MP Materials, Jim Litinsky, stated during an earnings call that "humanoid robots will have double or triple the magnetic content of an EV," as each robot requires "dozens of actuators."2

China Northern falls within the humanoid body bucket in KOID, specifically the "critical materials" subset, and supplies the core building block for humanoids: raw materials.

2. Schaeffler AG – 2.19% Weight in KOID as of 2/28/2026, +134.64% return since KOID inception1

Schaeffler (SFFLY) is a German motion technology company best known for its bearings, gearboxes, and actuators used in automotive and industrial applications. In humanoid robotics, Schaeffler supplies compact rotary gearboxes, linear actuators, and specialized bearings that sit inside joints such as shoulders, elbows, knees, and wrists, enabling precise, low-friction, and durable motion.

Within KOID’s framework, Schaeffler falls into the humanoid body bucket, specifically actuation and mechanical systems, as its components translate control signals into physical movement and load-bearing capacity for humanoid limbs.

3. DOOSAN CO LTD – 2.89% Weight in KOID as of 2/28/2026, +134.07% return since KOID inception1

Doosan is a Korean industrial group with significant exposure to industrial and collaborative robots through its Doosan Robotics business, as well as broader industrial equipment and automation solutions. Its robotic arms and automation platforms are natural stepping stones toward humanoid deployment in factories and logistics settings, leveraging shared technologies in actuators, control systems, and safety architectures.

In KOID, Doosan is best characterized as a humanoid integrator/body name: it sits closer to the system-integration and manufacturing layer while still being deeply tied into the mechanical and actuation stack leveraged by humanoid platforms.

4. Lynas Rare Earths – 2.85% Weight in KOID as of 2/28/2026, +130.62% return since KOID inception1

Like KOID holdings MP Materials and China Northern Rare Earth, Lynas Rare Earths (LYSCF) is a mineral and precious stone mining company supporting emerging technologies. However, Lynas is headquartered in Australia and mines rare earth minerals, including cerium, praseodymium, and neodymium. Lynas also plays a key role in the humanoid ecosystem, as its products are needed in the production of certain humanoid components and body parts.

Like its two portfolio mates, Lynas falls within the humanoid body bucket in KOID, specifically the "critical materials" subset, and supplies the core building block for humanoids: raw materials.

5. Nabtesco Corp. – 2.42% Weight in KOID as of 2/28/2026, +117.13% return since KOID inception1

Nabtesco (NCTKF) is a Japanese precision motion company best known for its RV-series precision reduction gears, which have a strong presence in the global market for industrial robot joint drives. These compact, rigid, low-backlash reducers are increasingly being adapted for humanoid hips, knees, and other high-load joints, where smooth, accurate, and durable motion is needed.

Within KOID, Nabtesco is a clear-cut contributor to the humanoid body, specifically in actuation and mechanical systems, providing the gear trains that sit at the heart of many robot joints.

6. Zhejiang Sanhua Intelligent Controls – 1.73% Weight in KOID as of 2/28/2026, +95.61% return since KOID inception1

Zhejiang Sanhua Intelligent Controls (ZSICY) is a China-based company that specializes in thermal management and HVAC control components, supplying valves, heat exchangers, pumps, and integrated thermal modules for home appliances and new energy vehicles. For humanoid robots, efficient thermal management is increasingly critical, as high-power actuators, dense electronics, and batteries generate heat that must be precisely controlled to maintain performance and reliability. Sanhua’s heat transfer and temperature-control solutions are a natural fit for humanoids.

In KOID, Sanhua sits in the humanoid body bucket, providing critical components and subsystems for cooling and thermal regulation of actuators, power electronics, and compute.

7. Leader Harmonious Drive Systems – 2.19% Weight in KOID as of 2/28/2026, +89.07% return since KOID inception1

Leader Harmonious Drive Systems is a Chinese motion-control manufacturer focused on strain-wave (harmonic) reducers and related precision drive components. Harmonic reducers are a core part of humanoid joints: they convert high-speed motor rotation into low-speed, high-torque, zero-backlash motion in compact, lightweight packages, enabling arms, wrists, and hands to execute smooth, precise, human-like movements.

This positions Leader Harmonious Drive Systems squarely in the humanoid body bucket as a key supplier of joint transmission and actuation hardware across the humanoid skeleton.

8. Moog Inc – 2.42% Weight in KOID as of 2/28/2026, +83.35% return since KOID inception1

Moog (MOG.A)(MOG.B) is a U.S. motion-control specialist that designs high-performance actuation systems, including electric, hydraulic, and hybrid actuators used in aerospace, industrial automation, and advanced robotics. The company’s integrated smart actuators and human-scale robotics solutions deliver high power density, precise force and position control, and strong operation in unstructured environments, making them well-suited for humanoid legs, arms, and exoskeleton-like systems.

Moog belongs to KOID’s humanoid body bucket as an actuation systems provider enabling dynamic, high-torque motion in humanoid platforms.

9. Magna International – 2.08% Weight in KOID as of 2/28/2026, +73.44% return since KOID inception1

The Chinese firm Magna International (MGA)(MG:CA) is traditionally an auto parts company that offers body exteriors, chassis structures, transmissions, lighting, seating, and fuel systems to customers worldwide. However, Magna is a "mechanical systems" specialist in KOID's humanoid ecosystem. Magna participates in humanoid mechanical systems primarily through its strategic partnership and investment in Sanctuary AI, under which it will help pilot the Phoenix general-purpose humanoid robots for deployment in its automotive factories.

In KOID’s bucketed approach, Magna falls within the humanoid body category, enabling the deployment of humanoids in its automotive factories and the supply of parts to humanoid manufacturers.

10. Jiangsu Hengli Hydraulic – 1.80% Weight in KOID as of 2/28/2026, +68.83% return since KOID inception1

Jiangsu Hengli Hydraulic is a Chinese manufacturer of hydraulic components and systems, including cylinders, valves, pumps, and, more recently, screw-type linear actuators. Its emerging linear actuator products are viewed as core hardware for humanoid robots, converting rotational energy into precise linear motion for joints and load-bearing mechanisms.

As such, Hengli sits firmly in KOID’s humanoid body bucket, supplying actuators and motion subsystems to the broader humanoid value chain, particularly within China’s growing component ecosystem.

Conclusion

Humanoid robotics may still be in the early innings, but the companies powering the “brains” and “bodies” of these systems are already creating meaningful value across global markets, from critical materials and precision actuators to advanced motion control and thermal management. By focusing on this enabling component layer and by accessing innovators across the U.S., Europe, Japan, Korea, and China, KOID offers a differentiated, equal-weighted approach to gaining exposure to the humanoid robotics and embodied intelligence ecosystem.

This material is provided for informational purposes only and is not intended as investment advice or a recommendation to buy or sell any security or investment product. The companies referenced represent holdings of the ETF at a point in time and are subject to change. Inclusion of a security should not be construed as an endorsement or indication of future performance.

High short-term performance from a limited number of the fund’s holdings is unusual, and investors should not expect such performance to be continued over the long term. Past performance does not guarantee future results.
